proof_stages.label: Unoriented skein cubes as a bridge
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces Khovanov homology, Heegaard/link Floer homology, and the Ozsváth-Szabó principle that an unoriented skein exact triangle can be iterated over the cube of resolutions to produce a spectral sequence whose first page is identified with the Khovanov complex.

proof_stages.label: Rasmussen conjecture as the target
proof_stages.summary: Rasmussen's conjectured rank inequality and spectral sequence from reduced Khovanov homology to knot Floer homology is presented, together with its unknot-detection consequence and the limitations of Dowlin's proof and the Kronheimer-Mrowka instanton analogue.

proof_stages.label: Obstruction to using ordinary link Floer homology
proof_stages.summary: The desired Heegaard-Floer-theoretic link invariant is required to have an unoriented skein exact triangle and to recover knot Floer homology on knots, but ordinary link Floer homology is shown not to satisfy the needed unoriented triangle.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem: unoriented link Floer homologies
proof_stages.summary: The speaker announces the invariants HF^natural, HF^sharp, and HF^double-sharp, their unoriented skein exact triangles and spectral sequences from reduced, unreduced, and minus Khovanov homology, and the identification HF^natural(K)=HFK-hat(K) for knots, giving Rasmussen's conjecture over F_2 while aligning structurally with instanton invariants.

proof_stages.label: Khovanov minus template and basepoint quotients
proof_stages.summary: The minus, unreduced, and reduced versions of Khovanov homology are reviewed through the basepoint action and quotient operations, providing the algebraic pattern that the new Heegaard Floer definitions will imitate.

proof_stages.label: Full basepointed link Floer complex
proof_stages.summary: The most general link Floer complex is recalled from a multi-pointed Heegaard diagram as a Lagrangian intersection Floer complex over variables attached to z- and w-basepoints, with basepoint divisors weighting holomorphic-curve counts and products defining homotopic U-actions.

proof_stages.label: Definition of the unoriented Floer variants
proof_stages.summary: The new complexes are defined algebraically by setting z_i=w_i=u_i^{1/2} to obtain the double-sharp version, killing a U-variable for the sharp version, and killing a distinguished basepoint action for the natural/reduced version.

examples.name: Hopf link skein test
examples.note: The Hopf link is used first to visualize unoriented resolutions, then as the rank counterexample showing ordinary link Floer homology cannot satisfy the needed unoriented triangle, and finally as a sanity check that the modified theory has the desired smaller rank.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker resolves the Hopf link in the skein triangle and later explicitly builds its Heegaard diagram, generators, differentials, quotients, and resulting ranks

passes.reason: The Hopf link is the minimal nontrivial two-component link and is used to show ordinary link Floer homology cannot satisfy the desired unoriented skein exact triangle because the ranks 4,1,1 violate exactness.
the Hopf link is the minimal nontrivial two-component link, and its skein ranks 4,1,1 are used to rule out an unoriented skein exact triangle for ordinary link Floer homology
The Hopf link is the minimal nontrivial linked two-component link, used here because its skein triple ranks obstruct an unoriented exact triangle for ordinary link Floer homology.
The Hopf link is the minimal nontrivial two-component link, and its 4,1,1 link-Floer ranks are used to obstruct the proposed unoriented skein exact triangle.
The Hopf link skein triple is used to show ordinary link Floer homology cannot satisfy an unoriented skein exact triangle, since the ranks 4,1,1 violate exactness.

The Hopf link is the minimal nontrivial two-component linking case, used by its ranks 4,1,1 against two unknot resolutions to rule out an unoriented skein exact triangle for ordinary link Floer homology.

The Hopf link skein triple is used to show ordinary link Floer homology cannot have an unoriented skein exact triangle, since ranks 4,1,1 violate exactness; the Hopf link is the minimal nontrivial two-component link.

The Hopf-link skein triple is used to show ordinary link Floer homology cannot have the desired unoriented exact triangle, and the Hopf link is the minimal highly regular nontrivial two-component link.
the minimal Hopf-link skein triple has HFL ranks 4,1,1, so it obstructs an unoriented skein exact triangle
the Hopf link is a highly symmetric minimal nontrivial link used as a rank obstruction to an unoriented skein exact triangle
Used to obstruct the ordinary link Floer unoriented skein triangle since the Hopf link and its unknot resolutions have ranks 4,1,1; structurally it is the highly symmetric minimal nontrivial link.

The Hopf-link skein triple is used to rule out an unoriented exact triangle for ordinary link Floer homology via ranks 4,1,1, and the Hopf link is the minimal nontrivial highly regular two-component link.

examples.name: Unknot rank comparison
examples.note: The unknot illustrates why Rasmussen's conjectured spectral sequence would imply Khovanov unknot detection, and later illustrates the rank difference between reduced and unreduced Khovanov homology.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states the unknot rank facts and uses them as a comparison point, but does not compute the unknot homology itself

passes.reason: The unknot is the trivial genus-zero base case with minimal rank, used for a detection/rank comparison rather than to refute a claim.
the unknot is the trivial minimal-genus knot used as the rank-one baseline in the detection/rank comparison, not to refute a claim
The unknot is the trivial genus-zero/minimal-crossing knot with minimal rank, used as a benchmark for detection rather than to refute a claim.
The unknot is the trivial minimum-genus base knot with rank-one homologies, used as an equality/base comparison rather than a refutation.
The unknot is the trivial/minimal knot and is used as the rank-one baseline for detection, not to refute a claim.
The unknot is the trivial minimal-genus/crossing knot with rank-one reduced homology, used as a detection benchmark rather than as a refutation.
The unknot is used as the rank-one base case in the detection argument, not to refute a claim; structurally it is the trivial/minimal genus-zero knot.
The unknot is the trivial genus-zero knot with minimal homological rank and round-circle symmetry, used as a benchmark for detection rather than as a refutation.
the unknot is the trivial genus-zero knot used as the minimal-rank comparison case
the unknot is the trivial minimal knot used as the rank-one base case rather than to refute a claim
Used as the base rank comparison in the unknot-detection argument, and the unknot is the trivial genus-zero minimal-crossing knot.
The unknot is the trivial, crossing/genus-minimal and maximally regular knot used as the rank-one benchmark, not as a failure case.

examples.name: Large computed knots and torsion evidence
examples.note: In response to a question, computations of knot Floer homology for knots with many crossings are cited as empirical evidence for the conjecture that knot Floer homology has no torsion.
examples.engagement_reason: large 40-crossing knot computations are cited only as evidence for absence of torsion, with no specific computation carried out

passes.reason: The large computed knots are ordinary high-crossing examples used as supporting evidence for torsion-freeness, not as structural exceptions or counterexamples.
